flashcards.back: Algorithm analysis is the process of determining the computational complexity of an algorithm - how the time and space requirements grow as the input size increases.
Time complexity measures the amount of time an algorithm takes to complete as a function of input size, typically expressed using asymptotic notations.
Space complexity measures the amount of memory an algorithm needs to hold data during execution, including input storage and auxiliary space.
Big O notation f(n) = O(g(n)) means there exist positive constants c and n₀ such that 0 ≤ f(n) ≤ c·g(n) for all n ≥ n₀. It represents the upper bound (worst-case).
Big Omega notation f(n) = Ω(g(n)) means there exist positive constants c and n₀ such that 0 ≤ c·g(n) ≤ f(n) for all n ≥ n₀. It represents the lower bound (best-case).
Big Theta notation f(n) = Θ(g(n)) means there exist positive constants c₁, c₂ and n₀ such that 0 ≤ c₁·g(n) ≤ f(n) ≤ c₂·g(n) for all n ≥ n₀. It represents tight bound.
Worst-case analysis considers the maximum time or space an algorithm needs for any input of size n, providing an upper bound guarantee.
Best-case analysis considers the minimum time or space an algorithm needs for any input of size n, providing a lower bound performance.
Average-case analysis considers the expected time or space over all possible inputs of size n, requiring knowledge of input distribution.
Input size is a measure of the amount of data the algorithm processes, typically the number of elements in an array, characters in a string, or vertices in a graph.

flashcards.front: List common time complexities in increasing order
flashcards.back: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ) < O(n!) - from constant to factorial time.
Asymptotic notation describes the limiting behavior of a function as the argument approaches infinity, used to express algorithm efficiency without exact counts.
O(1) means constant time - the algorithm takes the same amount of time regardless of input size. Examples: array index access, hash table lookup.
O(n) means linear time - the algorithm's time grows directly in proportion to input size. Examples: linear search, traversing an array.

flashcards.front: What does O(log n) complexity mean?
flashcards.back: O(log n) means logarithmic time - time grows slowly as input increases. Typical of divide-and-conquer algorithms like binary search.
